title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Quick Start

Course Schedule - Weekly schedule and course materials
Course Syllabus - Detailed course information and policies
Lectures - All course lectures organized by week
Assessments - Quizzes, exams, and projects
Resources - Textbook, R downloads, and reference materials

title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Course Overview
STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics (4 units) introduces descriptive statistics, probability, random variables, distributions, confidence intervals, hypothesis testing, regression, and ANOVA with emphasis on applications in biological and natural resource sciences.
Lecture: Monday, Wednesday, Friday, 11:00–11:50 AM, BSS 166
Lab: Thursday (select one section: 9:00–10:50 AM, 11:00–12:50 PM, or 1:00–2:50 PM), FH 202
General Education: Lower Division GE Area 2 (Quantitative Reasoning)

title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Course Structure
Includes collaborative daily warm-ups, weekly programming labs, weekly quizzes, two midterms, and five portfolio ready projects. Optional final exam may replace one midterm grade.

title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Instructor Information
Instructor: Dr. Rosanna Overholser
Office: BSS 334
Email: rho3@humboldt.edu
Office Hours: Mondays 2-3pm, Wednesdays 12-12:50pm, Fridays 10-10:50am, or by appointment

title: Midterm 2
text:
Midterm 2 will have two part style similar Midterm 1: a take-home computing with R component and an in-class component built from research questions and provided R output. All in-class questions will be drawn from the practice problems provided here.
Midterm 2 covers material after Midterm 1 through many-group / many-category inference (e.g. one-way ANOVA for several means; chi-squared for tables with several categories) and includes sampling, confidence intervals, hypothesis tests, one variable summaries/plots, two-variable summaries/plots , and study design.
Any resource may be used on the take-home component while you will be provided with a “methods table” during the in-class component. You may also use a scientific calculator during the in-class exam.



Please fill in the document provided in Canvas using the dataset there.

Univariate plots (tidyverse / ggplot2). For each numerical variable you analyze, include at least one appropriate plot (e.g. histogram or dot plot). For each categorical variable you analyze, include an appropriate plot (e.g. bar chart of counts).
Bivariate plots. For each pair of variables the take-home prompt tells you to relate, include one appropriate plot (e.g. scatterplot for two numerical variables; side-by-side boxplots or faceted histograms for numerical vs binary/categorical; stacked or grouped bar chart for two categorical variables).
Univariate descriptive statistics. For numerical variables: \(n\), mean, SD, median, IQR (or what the prompt asks). For categorical variables: counts and proportions (overall or as directed).
Bivariate descriptive statistics. For example: grouped means/SDs, two-way table of counts, joint or row proportions—whatever matches the variable types and the prompt.
Use t.test or prop.test to obtain a p-value or confidence interval as appropriate for the number and type of variables in the research question.




For each numbered scenario below you will see:

A short study description
A research question.
A fixed checklist of questions you should be prepared to answer.
Provided R code and output for that scenario.

Fixed checklist (repeat for every scenario).

Parameter. What population quantity is the question mainly about? Define it in words and symbols (e.g. use \(\mu\), \(p\), \(\mu_1 - \mu_2\) as appropriate and say what they represent in context in a sentence).
Variables. How many variables, and what are their types (numerical or categorical)?
Purpose. Is a confidence interval, a hypothesis test, or descriptive statistics and plots only most appropriate for the stated research question and design? Give one sentence why.
Descriptive Statistics: name one plot and one numerical summary you use to describe the variable(s) in the dataset that will be used to answer the research question.
By hand, be prepared to sketch a histogram, boxplot, bar plot or 100% stacked bar plot from a small dataset. Also be prepared to compute the mean, median, IQR, range, 5-number summary and proportions by row or column by hand from a small dataset.
If a test: state \(H_0\) and \(H_a\) (symbols + one sentence each in context). List conditions you would check for the procedure you named and say whether they are met for the provided sample (with reasons).
If a CI only: state the parameter the interval estimates and list conditions for the interval method you named; say whether they are met.
After output is provided: interpret the most appropriate printed statistic or plot and, if appropriate, a confidence interval, or p-value in context. Write a one-sentence conclusion. When appropriate, also name study design and sampling method (e.g. RCT with random assignment and convenience sample) and use these to whether causal language is appropriate and whether the results from the study generalize to a larger population.




Study. A set of \(n = 42\) coordinates were randomly selected from a grid of coordinates in a forest. The closest Douglas-fir seedling to each of these coordinates had it’s stem diameter (mm) measured
Research Question With high confidence, what is the mean stem diameter (mm) of all Douglas-fir seedings in this forest?


# A tibble: 10 × 1
   stem_mm
     <dbl>
 1     6.3
 2     7.1
 3     7.8
 4     8.1
 5     6.5
 6     7.2
 7     7.7
 8     5.3
 9     6.3
10     7  


# A tibble: 1 × 3
  `mean(stem_mm)` `sd(stem_mm)` `n()`
            <dbl>         <dbl> <int>
1            6.84          1.14    42










    One Sample t-test

data:  A$stem_mm
t = 38.747, df = 41, p-value < 2.2e-16
alternative hypothesis: true mean is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 6.479424 7.192005
sample estimates:
mean of x 
 6.835714 






Study. During one morning shift at a local hospital the wait time to triage in minutes was recorded for the 55 patients who arrived between 6am and 11am.
Research question. Is the mean wait time of all patients at this hospital greater than 20 minutes?


# A tibble: 11 × 1
   wait_min
      <dbl>
 1      7.6
 2     10.5
 3      6.5
 4     15  
 5     15.1
 6     16.8
 7     16.6
 8     10.3
 9     10.7
10     16.5
11     17.2


# A tibble: 1 × 3
  `mean(wait_min)` `sd(wait_min)` `n()`
             <dbl>          <dbl> <int>
1             16.9           9.92    55










    One Sample t-test

data:  B$wait_min
t = -2.2997, df = 54, p-value = 0.9873
alternative hypothesis: true mean is greater than 20
95 percent confidence interval:
 14.68799      Inf
sample estimates:
mean of x 
 16.92545 






Study. In Feburary of 2024, adults with mild hypertension who visited a particular rural clinic were randomly assigned to a daily walking coach app (Treatment) or a pamphlet (Control). After 8 weeks, systolic blood pressure (mmHg) was measured once per person.
Research question. Is there a difference in mean systolic blood pressure between adults who are given a daily walking coach app vs. who are given a pamphlet?


# A tibble: 10 × 2
   group     sbp
   <fct>   <dbl>
 1 Control   130
 2 Control   131
 3 Control   140
 4 Control   137
 5 Control   128
 6 Control   125
 7 Control   139
 8 Control   139
 9 Control   147
10 Control   125


# A tibble: 2 × 4
  group         n mean_sbp sd_sbp
  <fct>     <int>    <dbl>  <dbl>
1 Control      38     132.   9.36
2 Treatment    38     125.   7.29










    Welch Two Sample t-test

data:  sbp by group
t = 3.938, df = 69.79, p-value = 0.0001922
alternative hypothesis: true difference in means between group Control and group Treatment is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
  3.740327 11.417568
sample estimates:
  mean in group Control mean in group Treatment 
               132.4474                124.8684 






Study. Data was collected from first-year students at Cal Poly Humboldt in a section of calculus who chose to enroll in an optional calculus study workshop (Yes/No) in Fall of 2025. The final exam score in was recorded for all first-year students in that section of Calculus at the end of term.
Research question. What is the difference in mean final exam score for all first year students in Calculus who chose to enroll in the workshop and don’t?


# A tibble: 12 × 2
   workshop exam_score
   <fct>         <dbl>
 1 No             76.9
 2 No             92.4
 3 No             73.4
 4 No             71.1
 5 Yes            75.8
 6 Yes            70.2
 7 No             63.9
 8 No             67.6
 9 Yes            81.2
10 No             69.5
11 Yes            76.3
12 Yes            91.4


# A tibble: 2 × 4
  workshop     n  mean    sd
  <fct>    <int> <dbl> <dbl>
1 No          52  71.2  6.75
2 Yes         38  81.1  7.06










    Welch Two Sample t-test

data:  exam_score by workshop
t = -6.7023, df = 77.798, p-value = 2.914e-09
alternative hypothesis: true difference in means between group No and group Yes is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 -12.853011  -6.965815
sample estimates:
 mean in group No mean in group Yes 
         71.18269          81.09211 


# A tibble: 4 × 5
# Groups:   fulljob [2]
  fulljob         workshop     n  mean    sd
  <fct>           <fct>    <int> <dbl> <dbl>
1 No fulltime job No          19  77.7  5.38
2 No fulltime job Yes         28  84.5  3.63
3 Fulltime job    No          33  67.4  4.07
4 Fulltime job    Yes         10  71.5  5.14













Study. Suppose that a graduate student student has access to \(n = 48\) agricultural plots in rural Kentucky. For each plot, they measured soil nitrate (mg/kg) and leaf nitrogen (%) once in June of 2025.
Research question. Describe the relationship between soil nitrate and leaf nitrogen in this dataset and whether a linear trend is plausible. (Hint: remember to address form, direction, strength)


# A tibble: 8 × 2
  soil_nitrate leaf_n_pct
         <dbl>      <dbl>
1           32        1  
2           51        1.1
3          100        2.3
4           32        0.9
5           99        2.3
6           54        1.2
7           26        0.8
8          107        2.3













Study. Suppose that in a large company of 50,893 employees, 36 were randomly chosen to wear an accelerometer for one workday. From these devices, the minutes each employee was seated was calculated and the employees were also ask to self-report fatigue on a scale of 0–10.
Research question. Describe the relationship between minutes seated and fatigue score in this sample of 36 employees.


# A tibble: 8 × 2
  minutes_seated fatigue_0_10
           <dbl>        <dbl>
1            387          6.3
2            511         10  
3            244          5.5
4            424          9  
5            365          8.3
6            446          8.6
7            312          7  
8            256          5.2













Study. In a study of flu vaccines, 120 healthy adult volunteers were randomly assigned to vaccine or placebo in the fall of 2024. Whether or not they got the flu (yes/no) during the next 3 months of follow-up was recorded.
Research question. Does the population rate of flu differ between adults who get the vaccine or not?



    2-sample test for equality of proportions without continuity correction

data:  c(xa, xb) out of c(na, nb)
X-squared = 9.2308, df = 1, p-value = 0.00238
alternative hypothesis: two.sided
95 percent confidence interval:
 0.04898991 0.21767675
sample estimates:
    prop 1     prop 2 
0.20000000 0.06666667 


# A tibble: 4 × 3
# Groups:   arm [2]
  arm     flu       n
  <fct>   <fct> <int>
1 Placebo No       96
2 Placebo Yes      24
3 Vaccine No      112
4 Vaccine Yes       8













Study. Students from engineering programs two universities were randomly selected to have their resumes evaluated by their universities’ career services clinic before going to their local career fair: Several weeks after the career fair, the students were asked if they accepted a summer internship offer (yes/no) from an employer at the fair.
Research question. With high confidence, estimate the difference in internship offer rate based the career services resume feedback between the two universities in the populations of all engineering students.



    2-sample test for equality of proportions without continuity correction

data:  c(sum(H$offer[H$uni == "Timber"]), sum(H$offer[H$uni == "Wild Horse"])) out of c(n1, n2)
X-squared = 4.6339, df = 1, p-value = 0.03135
alternative hypothesis: two.sided
95 percent confidence interval:
 -0.28498163 -0.01501837
sample estimates:
prop 1 prop 2 
  0.34   0.49 


# A tibble: 4 × 3
# Groups:   uni [2]
  uni        offer     n
  <fct>      <fct> <int>
1 Timber     No       66
2 Timber     Yes      34
3 Wild Horse No       51
4 Wild Horse Yes      49













Study. Suppose that farmers were invited to participate in a study of soybean growth. The farmers who participated were located within one of three regions (North / Central / South). Yield (bushels per acre) was measured at harvest.
Research question. Is mean yield the same across all three region?


# A tibble: 3 × 4
  region      n mean_yield sd_yield
  <fct>   <int>      <dbl>    <dbl>
1 North      22       49.4     4.18
2 Central    20       54.6     4.04
3 South      21       49.2     5.52









Analysis of Variance Table

Response: yield_bu_ac
          Df  Sum Sq Mean Sq F value    Pr(>F)    
region     2  375.42 187.708  8.7576 0.0004602 ***
Residuals 60 1286.01  21.434                      
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1






Study. A healthcare system manager obtained records from four clinics (A, B, C, D) on whether their patients returned for a follow-up visit within 30 days (yes/no) during 2025.
Research question. Is return rate differ between at least two of the four clinics for all patients in a similar time frame?


# A tibble: 8 × 3
# Groups:   clinic [4]
  clinic returned     n
  <fct>  <fct>    <int>
1 A      No          10
2 A      Yes         44
3 B      No          17
4 B      Yes         45
5 C      No          26
6 C      Yes         31
7 D      No          10
8 D      Yes         37










    Pearson's Chi-squared test

data:  tabJ
X-squared = 12.035, df = 3, p-value = 0.007263
